entry.title: Striking a Balance: Navigating the Open-Source Tightrope in a World of Evolving Threats - Part 1
entry.content:
By Philip Engelmartin, Technical Advisor, Office of the CSO

 

Understanding the Multifaceted Risks of Open-Source Reliance

Open-source software (OSS) is integral to the software development landscape, renowned for its perceived cost savings, innovation potential, and community collaboration. Nonetheless, heavy reliance on OSS introduces significant risks and complexities that organizations must thoughtfully navigate. This article delves into the numerous risks tied to OSS and underscores the necessity of addressing these challenges to build a more secure and resilient software supply chain.

The Open and Collaborative Nature of OSS: Both Asset and Liability

The open and collaborative nature of OSS development is a double-edged sword. While it drives rapid innovation and promotes community engagement, it also opens doors to vulnerabilities that malicious actors can exploit. These threats extend beyond simple coding errors and manifest in various insidious forms:

Malicious Contributors

One of the most significant risks is the potential for threat actors to disguise themselves as legitimate contributors and inject malicious code directly into OSS projects. Given the decentralized and often anonymous nature of OSS contributions, it becomes challenging to properly vet contributors. Consequently, the introduction and downstream propagation of malicious code become significant risks, compromising the security and integrity of software. The lack of formalized vetting processes in many OSS communities exacerbates this issue, making it easier for bad actors to slip under the radar.

Hidden Backdoors and Logic Bombs

Malicious code can be cunningly hidden within seemingly benign functionalities, activated by a remote command, a specific date, or an event. These concealed threats can leak sensitive data, disrupt operations, or provide unauthorized access to critical systems, potentially causing extensive damage before detection and remediation. The complexity and vast size of many OSS projects make it easier to hide such malicious code, increasing the risk that it will go unnoticed until it is too late.

Supply Chain Vulnerabilities

OSS projects often depend on a complex web of other open-source components, creating a vast attack surface. A single compromised component can trigger cascading effects throughout the ecosystem. Adding to this complexity is the issue of crypto agility - the ability to swiftly adapt to changes in cryptographic algorithms and implementations. Many OSS components rely on cryptography for security but may not be designed for easy upgrades or transitions, leaving software vulnerable [1]. The interconnectedness of OSS projects and their dependencies makes it difficult to track and manage vulnerabilities effectively.

Zero-Day Exploits and Public Disclosure

Vulnerabilities found in OSS are often disclosed publicly, giving malicious actors a window of opportunity to exploit these flaws before patches are developed and deployed. This race against time leaves organizations vulnerable to attacks that exploit these known weaknesses, highlighting the critical importance of prompt and effective vulnerability management [2]. Public disclosure also means that organizations must be ready to act quickly to mitigate risks, which can be challenging given the widespread use of OSS components.

The Log4Shell Incident: A Wake-Up Call

The Log4Shell vulnerability [3], discovered in the widely used Log4j logging library, serves as a stark reminder of the extensive impact a single OSS vulnerability can have. Affecting thousands of organizations globally, this incident underscores the urgent need for a more secure and controlled approach to software development. It emphasizes the necessity of proactive measures and robust security practices to protect against such widespread vulnerabilities that can devastate an organization's security posture.

Unpredictable Lifecycles and Support Gaps

The decentralized and often volunteer-driven nature of OSS development can lead to unpredictable project lifecycles, creating significant challenges for organizations that rely on these components:

Maintenance Gaps and Security Risks

Unsupported or abandoned OSS components are less likely to receive timely security updates, leaving software vulnerable to exploits. This lack of maintenance extends to cryptographic components as well. Without active development, OSS projects may fall behind in implementing the latest cryptographic standards or addressing newly discovered vulnerabilities in existing algorithms, exacerbating security risks. Organizations must invest significant resources in monitoring and maintaining these components to mitigate these risks.

Compatibility Issues and Integration Challenges

Combining OSS components with different versions and dependencies can lead to compatibility issues, requiring considerable engineering effort and potentially impacting software stability. These challenges necessitate rigorous testing and continuous integration practices to ensure seamless and stable software performance. Organizations must be prepared to dedicate substantial resources to managing these integration challenges, which can be particularly burdensome in complex software environments.

Lack of Service Level Agreements (SLAs)

OSS typically does not come with the formal support and SLAs provided by commercial software. This absence leaves organizations to rely on internal resources or community support, which can be inconsistent and unreliable, particularly when dealing with critical issues that demand immediate resolution. Organizations must often invest in building internal expertise to manage these support gaps, which can be a significant drain on resources and expertise.

Conclusion

While OSS offers many benefits, the risks associated with its use can be substantial and often underestimated. Organizations must carefully assess these risks and take proactive steps to mitigate them. Understanding the multifaceted risks is the first step in developing a comprehensive strategy to build a more secure and resilient software supply chain. This approach requires organizations to strike a delicate balance between leveraging the advantages of OSS and implementing robust security measures to protect against evolving threats.

Stay tuned for the next article in this series, where we explore the complexities of OSS licensing and geopolitical influences. We'll also be sharing how we're addressing the associated risks within our own use of open-source software in a later blog article.

[1] https://sweet32.info/  
[2] https://www.threatdown.com/blog/five-years-later-heartbleed-vulnerability-still-unpatched/  
[3] https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/cve-2021-44228

entry.title: Adapting to a New Reality: SAP’s Access Governance Solutions
entry.content:
With the rise of AI, cloud adoption, and stricter compliance demands, access governance—especially with fine-grained Segregation of Duties (SoD) analysis—has become essential. Industries such as finance, healthcare, and government face strict regulations (e.g., GDPR, SOX, HIPAA) that require safeguarding sensitive data and controlling user access. Access governance solutions are vital in ensuring compliance and security, reducing the risk of fraud, errors, and data breaches. 

Unauthorized access remains a primary cause of data breaches, leading to significant financial and reputational damage. A 2024 Ponemon/ IBM report highlighted that the average cost of a data breach is $4.45 million. By implementing SoD analysis, businesses can mitigate such risks by ensuring that critical tasks are distributed among multiple individuals, preventing potential fraud or mismanagement. 

SAP customers are in the process of moving from on-premises as well as implementing new cloud solutions to take advantage of the benefits they offer. Access governance solutions are required as critical in each of these transformations in all geographic areas, industries, and organizational sizes. To support these varied customer adoption challenges, SAP offers two key solutions for access governance: SAP Access Control (SAP AC) and SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance (SAP Cloud IAG). These solutions cater to different business requirements, technology stacks, and implementation models. 

 

SAP Access Control: Flexible and Scalable for Large Enterprises  

SAP AC is ideal for large organizations with complex on-premises systems and hybrid environments. AC is available in both on-premise and private cloud deployment models. SAP Access Control has a combination of configuration options, APIs, and interfaces that enable extensive customization. The customization applies to each module and access governance process. Access Control has been implemented across SAP’s largest customers with broad application landscapes.

SAP Access Control Features 

  • Seamless Cloud Migration: SAP AC preserves custom configurations during cloud transitions, ensuring continued security and compliance. 
  • Fine-Grained SoD for Legacy & Modern Systems: SAP AC applies flexible SoD controls across both legacy and modern cloud-based systems. 
  • Compliance & Risk Management: It helps businesses manage SoD violations, ensuring adherence to regulations like SOX and GDPR. 
  • Flexible workflow engine – Based on SAP Business Workflow, this is highly flexible and customizable to handle different user provisioning and approval requirements. 
  • Extensive reporting – The solution offers a wide set out of the box reporting out of the box based on secured audit certifiable data.  
  • Emergency Access Management – Access Control includes a complete end-to-end solution for assigning temporary elevated permissions for urgent situations, complete with activity monitoring and post-session reviews.

 

SAP Cloud IAG: Designed for cloud - streamlined and optimized.  

SAP IAG is a cost-effective Saas solution that supports both on-premise and cloud applications with complete functionality and fast ROI. SAP Cloud IAG includes pre-configured templates, rulesets, and workflows with a simplified implementation process. IAG is built from the ground up on a multi-tenant Saas solution running on BTP. There is less to configure and once subscribed, customers get two tenants ready to run. All that’s necessary is to connect IAG with the business applications.  

There are two versions of SAP IAG – standard and integration edition. The standard edition supports full functionality while the integration edition is tailored as a cloud application extension for SAP Access Control. 

  • Faster Implementation: SAP Cloud IAG reduces deployment time with out-of-the-box access risk ruleset content and persona-based navigation. 
  • Scalability Across Hybrid Environments: It supports both cloud and on-premises environments, making it suitable for hybrid or multi-cloud strategies. 
  • Third-Party Integration: SAP Cloud IAG integrates with a broad range of third-party applications, ensuring consistent access governance across the entire IT landscape. 
  • Integrates/Extends Access Control – IAG, integration edition provides cloud system support for SAP Access Control. 
  • S4 Hana Public Cloud – The only access governance solution for SAP S4 Hana Public Cloud.
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services – Seamless integration with SAP Cloud Identity Services for security and broad integration with SAP enterprise applications.                       
 

Conclusion: Securing the Digital Future with SAP Access Governance 

With SAP's comprehensive solutions, SAP Access Control and SAP Cloud IAG, organizations can ensure secure, compliant, and efficient management of user access across both legacy and cloud systems. Whether it's large enterprises with complex hybrid environments or businesses adopting cloud-first strategies, SAP provides the tools needed to protect sensitive data, reduce access risk, and maintain regulatory compliance. By leveraging the right access governance tools, businesses can safeguard their operations and build trust in an increasingly interconnected digital landscape. 

 

More information  

  • SAP Access Control Product Page and Documentation 
  • SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance Product Page and Documentation

 

Authors 

  • Swetta Singh
  • Chris Radkowski

entry.title: New attack detection patterns released for SAP Enterprise Threat Detection, cloud edition
entry.content:
Seeing a pattern?

We're delighted to announce the latest delivery of 20 new patterns for SAP Enterprise Threat Detection, cloud edition, extending our library to nearly 100 patterns and use cases!

These additions fall into four key areas:

  1. System-Specific Security
    - Focusing on ABAP, S/4HANA, and HANA systems
    - Includes critical debugging monitoring, system changelogs, and data protection
  2. Attack Detection and Prevention
    - Covering various Denial of Service (DoS) attack scenarios
    - Monitoring web request anomalies across different endpoints and users
  3. Access and Authentication Monitoring
    - Detecting unusual login behaviors and user activities
    - Identifying potential credential abuse or sharing
  4. Network and Communication Security
    - Addressing encryption standards and deprecated protocols
    - Ensuring secure communication channels

These new patterns aren't just rebuilding of existing content from our private cloud code line. Far from it!

The new versions have been adapted to the changing requirements in increasingly cloud-based system landscapes and evolving threat landscape; enhanced based on productive experience; and refined with a focus increasing the relevance of resulting alerts out of the box.

It's worth highlighting the inherent flexibility of all ETD patterns. Each pattern acts as a "super pattern," encompassing whole categories of suspicious or threatening cases. Thanks to our normalized and semantic data structure, along with the use of value lists, we can evaluate complex n:n(:n:n...) relationships. This means a single pattern applies to a wide variety of scenarios.

Moreover, we're excited to share another enhancement to ETDc further enhancing the patterns' flexibility: the ability to incorporate exceptions. This new feature allows for even greater adaptability, enabling you to fine-tune pattern behavior to your specific environment and needs.

Details and documentation in the help pages (logon required):  

https://help.sap.com/whats-new/5899f0627ef14f8db6fe04d0c5753ab3?locale=en-US

What do you think about this addition? Happy about your comments!

SAP Enterprise Threat Detection 

@Lingscheid @i353gfiata @eugen_pritzkau @michael_schmitt6 @KirtiSingh01

entry.title: Kickstart your SAP GRC Learning Journey: Introduction to SAP GRC, Submodules and useful links
entry.content:
1. GRC Principles & Harmonization - Introduction to GRC (GRC100) To begin your SAP GRC journey, you'll start with the fundamentals. GRC100 will give you an introduction to the principles of Governance, Risk, and Compliance. This foundational module lays the groundwork for understanding how GRC helps businesses manage risks, ensure compliance, and streamline governance. Also helps to understand basic harmonized reporting framework.

2. SAP Access Control (GRC300) SAP Access Control is a crucial element for managing user access within an organization. In GRC300, you will learn how to implement and configure SAP Access Control to ensure that users have the right level of access to the right systems and data, minimizing security risks.

SAP Access Control has submodules such as Access Risk Analysis (ARA), Emergency Access Management (EAM), Access Request Management (ARM), Business Role Management (BRM) and Certifications – User Access Review (UAR) etc.

Reference SAP Standard Learning journey:

https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/discovering-the-main-functionalities-of-sap-access-control

3. SAP Process Control (GRC330) SAP Process Control is designed to improve compliance with regulations and internal policies. It helps to implement Internal Controls. In GRC330, you will dive into implementing Process Control with all required configurations. Process Control is used to automate and monitor controls, making it easier to manage processes and ensure compliance across your organization.

SAP Process Control has submodules such as Continuous Control Monitoring (CCM), Assessments, Surveys, Ad Hoc Issue Management, Policy Management and Sign-off.

Reference SAP Standard Learning journey:

https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/discovering-the-key-functionalities-of-sap-process-control

4. SAP Risk Management (GRC340) SAP Risk Management helps to identify, analyze, and mitigate risks. This module is essential for those looking to build a comprehensive risk management framework within their organization.

SAP Risk Management has key features/sub modules such as Risk Planning, Risk Identification, Risk Analysis, Risk Response and Risk Monitor and Reporting.

Reference SAP Standard Learning journey:

https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/exploring-sap-risk-management

5. SAP Business Integrity Screening (GRC350) SAP Business Integrity Screening (BIS) is a new name for SAP Fraud Management (FM). It helps to detect and prevent fraud by screening business partners against blacklists, watchlists, and other data sources, and it uses CDS views.

SAP Business Integrity Screening (BIS) is one of the modules in SAP Assurance compliance Software (ACS), it has key features/submodules such as Detection of Suspicious Patterns (using Detection Methods, Detection Strategies), Predictive solution using ML, Fuzzy logics etc.

6. SAP Tax Compliance (GRC360) SAP Tax compliance is a critical aspect of governance, especially for global organizations. It helps you to learn how to implement SAP Tax Compliance solutions to ensure that your organization adheres to local and international tax laws, reducing the risk of non-compliance.

SAP Tax Compliance is one of the modules in SAP Assurance Compliance Software (ACS), it has key features/sub modules such as managing compliance management etc.

7. SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance (GRC370) SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance (IAG) is a latest cloud solution for Access Governance. It offers similar features like On-Prem Access Control; however, it is still evolving. As most of the organizations shifting to the cloud, it’s essential to ensure secure identity and access management. It helps you control who has access to what in a cloud environment.

SAP Cloud IAG has submodules/services such as Access Analysis, Access Request, Access Certification, Role Design and Privileged Access Management.

8. SAP Audit Management (GRC380) SAP Audit Management helps to automate internal auditing procedures and improve quality.

It helps to setup end to end audit management and helps to plan, execute, and manage audits within your organization, ensuring that you're always prepared for internal and external scrutiny.

SAP Audit Management has key features/submodules such as Audit Planning, Audit Preparation, Audit Execution, Audit Reporting and Audit Follow-up.

I hope this helps to start your journey into SAP GRC also if you're aiming to level up your skills in SAP GRC ! By diving deep into these modules, you'll gain the knowledge and skills needed to excel in the world of Governance, Risk, and Compliance.

Please connect and follow me for the next upcoming informative articles in SAP Security, GRC, BTP, Internal Controls and Risk & Controls area.

Share your thoughts in the comments below !

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: What's New in SAP solutions for International Trade Management, Enhancing Compliance
entry.content:
“New trade sanctions become operational” is a headline that one can regularly read these days.

For many companies – especially in the financial sector or sensitive industries, this has been a reality for a long time. But more and more organizations are realizing that these compliance requirements apply to them because they have dealings in geographies or with stakeholders that are now in the scope of a sanction regime.

Let’s roll back and start with the basics:

 

What are sanctions?

 

In short, sanctions are political and economic decisions resulting in restrictions placed on trade and financial activities. Failure to comply creates an offence that might lead to penalty measures.

And the list of activities that constitutes an offense can be quite long.

For instance, “trading, importing, exporting, selling, purchasing, transferring, transiting or transporting goods” of course, but also “providing brokering services, technical assistance or other services”, “making funds or economic resources available directly or indirectly to, or for the benefit of, a designated person, entity or body”, “failing to freeze funds or economic resources belonging to or owned, held or controlled by a designated person, entity or body”, “providing financial services or performing financial activities”… and more are detailed in the 2024 update to the EU Directive on the definition of criminal offences and penalties for the violation of Union restrictive measures.  

There are currently 14 ongoing sanctions regimes enforced by the UN Security Council, but many states – or economic unions, have their own set of sanction measures addressing specific sectors, territories or individuals. This is the case for the United States, the European Union, Switzerland, the United Kingdom, China, Canada, or Australia just to name a few.

Enforcing these ever-evolving measures is by no means an easy task, and keeping track of all the changes adds to the complexity. But breaking these sanctions is definitely not an option as, at the very least, it will lead to bad reputation, but also fines, loss of license to operate or even criminal charges can be enforced.

And, with predefined easy to use content from Mendel Verlag, this is precisely what SAP Watch List Screening has been designed to support as I am sure you already know.

But there are also cases where companies want to screen against custom watch lists: either coming from a new content provider, or maybe even internal restrictions, potentially conflicting parties, etc.

 

Introducing Custom Watch List Uploading

 

With the November 2024 update, Product Management and Engineering teams for SAP solutions for International Trade Management have introduced a powerful new feature: the ability to upload custom watch list content. This new capability enables organizations to enhance their screening processes proactively, safeguarding the company against potential risks arising from being involved with dubious stakeholders.

You can now seamlessly integrate your own watch list content into our SAP Watch List Screening solution using an API.

This functionality allows users to:

Personalize Screening Processes

Tailor the screening to your organization’s specific needs by adding entities that are particularly relevant to your operations. For example, this includes lists of fraudulent taxpayers, or persons and organizations with whom you prefer not to do business

Stay Up-to-Date

Regularly update your custom watch lists to reflect the latest information pertinent to your industry or region

Leverage Additional Data Provider Content

Include lists from different providers, or additional lists provided by Mendel Verlag which are not included in the sanctioned party list screening packages offered

The upload and management of custom watch lists are entirely under your charge, giving you full control over the content you screen against.

To achieve this, a new user-friendly Configuration app has been released:

 With this new Fiori app, users can:

Configure Watch List Package Categories

 

Assign appropriate categories to the content provided by Mendel Verlag, ensuring accurate organization and screening

Manage Screening Settings

 

Decide whether your uploaded custom watch list should be active and used during the screening process, giving you full control over compliance operations

Monitor Watch List Fill Levels

 

Keep track of the volume and status of your uploaded custom watch lists to ensure they are comprehensive and effective

 

Getting Started

 

If you are already using SAP Watch List Screening and would like to leverage this new functionality, simply follow the steps below:

 

 

 

Prepare Your Custom Watch List Content

Gather the data you wish to include in your custom watch list. This could be entities you prefer to avoid, or additional lists from data providers

 

 

Use the API for Upload

Utilize SAP’s API to upload your custom watch list content into the SAP Watch List Screening system

 

 

Configure with the New App

Access the new app to set up your watch list package categories, determine screening usage, and monitor fill levels

 

Find out more information

 

Should you be interested in reading more, you will find additional details at the following pages:

  • Feature in What's New in SAP Watch List Screening
  • Dedicated section in the Administration Guide for SAP Watch List Screening
  • REST API description in the SAP Business Accelerator Hub

Credits for the information in this blog go to Valentin Kruck and David Sommer, leading SAP Watch List Screening for Product Management and Engineering at SAP.

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

entry.title: Beyond Prioritization: Combating Alert Fatigue Through Policy Enforcement at The Edge
entry.content:
By Jay Thoden van Velzen, Technical Advisor, Office of the CSO

Cybersecurity teams, developers and system operators increasingly struggle with alert fatigue. As new technologies, regulations and security tooling are rolled out, this comes with an increasing volume of security scans and events. The more investment is made in security programs, the more alerts these programs generate. Consequently, this can generate more remediation tasks than the organization can manage.

For illustration, research has shown that organizations have only the capacity to remediate 10% of the vulnerabilities in their environments within a given month. Teams are forced to prioritize what is most important to address.

 

The Security Alert Prioritization Funnel

To manage the volume of alerts, security engineering teams filter and enrich data, add contextualization, allowing us to prioritize alerts by criticality and urgency. They automate alerts where they can, to reduce the number of alerts to something that security- or other operations teams – whether Security Operations Center (SOC) analysts, operational or developer teams – can act on. This funneling takes a lot of effort, so it is here represented as a mountain to climb.

Initial filtering can be done on the raw security data sources of events. Not all logged events are necessarily relevant. Neither are all data elements of the logged event. Data enrichment with ownership and organization, and metadata associated with the resource where the event was raised contained in asset inventory management further contextualize the event with technical and business context. This allows for the prioritization of alerts based on where in the landscape architecture the event occurred, what the resource’s function is in the landscape and how critical to business operation. It also assigns which organization is responsible for the resource and who to contact to act on the alert.

Automated runbooks for autoremediation, response or containment, that is, Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response (SOAR), can further lighten the load of the human operators at the very end of the funnel or buy them time.

Anton’s Alert Fatigue: The Study (2024) by Dr. Anton Chuvakin gives a good overview of this type of funneling within the context of security detection and response, and outlines challenges. But the same applies also to security configuration and vulnerability management, or alerts generated through security scans in the development cycle. All add to the alert volume that teams need to respond to.

 

Scaling Challenges

The problem with this model is that ultimately this scales poorly. Assuming that alerts are tuned well, with low false positives and appropriately assigned and prioritized, when the volume of the security events increases, this also increases the alert volume assigned to teams, and an increase in alert fatigue.

 Reasons for such increases are:

  • Growth in the size of the landscape and growth in deployed resources
  • Adoption of new technology platforms, such as public cloud, Kubernetes, or Software-as-a-Services (SaaS) solutions
  • Deployment of new security tools in areas not previously covered
  • Operationalization of new detection rules

To handle this increased volume of alerts at the end of the funnel, you can make the business case for more SOC analysts, developers or DevOps engineers. More likely you will be asked to do more with less. Automation, including with the help of AI, makes human operators more efficient and effective. But as the volume of security events increases, eventually you will be forced to an ever more brutal prioritization to get alerts back to a manageable volume that people can handle.

For illustration, let’s assume that we have 10,000,000 security events within a week that after prioritization and automation leads to 10,000 critical and high-value alerts, or a funnel reduced to 0.1% of the raw events processed. With 100 people to address those, that is 100 critical and high alerts per person per week.

What do organizations do when the events grow to 20,000,000, 50,000,000, or 100,000,000? Do they hire more people? Do they automate more? Can they afford to only address critical alerts, and accept the risk of deprioritizing the highs? Do they focus only on vulnerabilities listed in the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) Catalog? How much can you prioritize before your security programs become security theater?

 

Policy Enforcement at the Edge

The enforcement of policies at the edge, that is, directly acting on resources that generate security events, can reduce both the volume of events to process, as well as reduce the latency between detection and remediating action.

Reduce Event Volume

If we can’t prioritize and optimize our way out of the problem, we must “cut” the volume of events going into the funnel – or at the very least, reduce their priority. Applying policies at the edge that guarantee secure configurations or ban certain behavior reduces the volume of events that must be processed, while still allowing for notification that the event has occurred but has been dealt with. That gives high-fidelity signals that something has been corrected but doesn’t require immediate action from anybody.

One such example of policy enforcement at the edge, or in the fabric of the platform, are SAP’s cloud guardrails. These guardrails enforce baseline secure configurations of cloud infrastructure when resources are created or modified and do much of the heavy lifting in SAP’s Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) program. Among other controls, these prevent publicly exposed Secure Shell (SSH) and Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) connections and allow such connections from known SAP IP ranges. That protects against operators unintentionally exposing such ports to attack but also stops malicious actors from exposing such services should they have gained a foothold in the environment in another way.

Cloud account owners are notified when these guardrails auto-remediate resources, allowing them to correct their deployment scripts, or raise an alarm when they find they didn’t make the change that triggered the action.

Other examples of edge or in-the-fabric policy enforcement are Admission Control in Kubernetes clusters, enforced image scans and build checks. While those are primarily to ensure secure software development and deployment, they also ensure once deployed that adversaries can’t, for instance:

  • Deploy overprivileged containers
  • Deploy containers that don’t come from approved image registries
  • Deploy containers that lack required code signing

That adds defense-in-depth barriers that add obstacles to potential attackers, increases cyber resilience, and buys security operations time to detect and respond.

Reduce Latency

When human operators need to intervene, the time to resolve an issue can be very long. Research from Bitsight indicates that organizations in 2023 took nearly 4.5 months to remediated critical severity KEV vulnerabilities, and more than 9 months for high severity. That is in line with research from Mend.io that found that on average it takes 271 days to fix a vulnerability. Well-resourced organizations may be able to reduce that to weeks, but that still leaves a window for exploitation.

Response to suspected detected security incidents will be much faster. But in high-velocity landscapes, resources may only exist for hours, minutes or seconds, and be destroyed by the time a human operator can react.

 Automation through the funnel pipeline has a latency of its own, just to get alerts into the hands of the right people or to drive an automated response. The collection and ingestion of events, data enrichment and contextualization, and prioritization of alerts all require data collection, pipelining and processing. The higher the event volume, the more computational time that takes. Your SOAR automation may not be fast enough if it has to wait for that processing to complete.

Policy enforcement at the edge, on the host or in the cloud fabric, and before going through this event data pipeline reduces response latency.

 

Challenges and More Sophisticated Controls

Edge policies are blunt instruments. They operate before adding organizational context, and without the nuance that that enables. To take the public SSH and RDP example again, that guardrail applies to any cloud account and workload, whether running customer solutions, internal use by development, or a team running a sandbox account for experiments. Edge policies only work where their impact doesn’t create an operational problem for legitimate use cases or cause outages. Exceptions for edge cases can be complex to implement. The more varied and complex your environment, the harder it may be to implement a broad policy that enforces a particular configuration or bans certain behavior from the landscape.

The rollout of edge policies also requires more care. The cloud guardrails are deployed in four “deployment rings” where over the course of a quarter an increasing number of cloud accounts are onboarded, to minimize unforeseen disruptions. By first deploying to test and pre-production accounts, issues are bound to emerge during testing before they impact production environments, and the policy can be corrected or even withdrawn from the rollout.

Other controls can be too risky to implement. Forced autorotation of encryption keys or downgrade of authorizations for a seemingly overprivileged admin or service account can have devastating downstream impact. Auto-updates of vulnerable software components can have unpredictable consequences when not managed through appropriate development and IT operations processes. It isn’t possible to eliminate the funnel altogether.

However, based on its effectiveness in CSPM, SAP is exploring if such edge controls can be taken further. Aside from autoremediating misconfigurations and deployment controls, we are researching whether certain behaviors can be banned from the landscape. Technologies like eBPF allow us to reconsider what is possible. This is primarily used for observability through its direct visibility into the Linux kernel, including system calls and network events, for both IT operations and security use cases.

But eBPF also allows for the interception of system calls and network events, and therefore the opportunity to apply policies to block them at runtime. This could potentially ban reverse shells from the landscape, by blocking the setup of network sockets to a remote address with an interactive shell. It could block code execution beyond a process’s defined process boundaries and namespace and thereby protect against several classes of vulnerability exploitation.

Edge policies protecting against this, displaying clear malicious behavior with no legitimate use, would provide an additional layer of defense against the consequences of an attempted exploit, while providing a high-confidence indication that the affected resource is under active attack. This could trigger a deeper investigation by SOC analysts, but under lower time and impact pressure than if the attack succeeded – let alone if it had gone undetected.

We look forward to making progress towards this in 2025.

 

More Information

  • The Hidden Complexity of Vulnerability Remediation: Bridging the Gap between Data and Common Advice, Wade Baker
  • Anton’s Alert Fatigue: The Study (Medium), Dr. Anton Chuvakin
  • Asset Inventory Management Foundational to Effective Security Governance (SAP Community)
  • Bitsight Reveals More than 60 Percent of Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Remain Unmitigated Past Deadlines in First-of-its-Kind Analysis of CISA’s KEV Catalog (Bitsight)
  • Securing the Software Supply Chain: Key Findings from the Mend Open Source Risk Report (Mend.io)
  • Cloud Guardrails: Effectively Making Your Cloud Accounts Secure-by-Default (SAP Community)
  • How SAP Automates Security Scanning and Alerting on over 6,000 AWS Accounts (SAP Community)
  • What is eBPF? (eBPF.io)

entry.title: The Broad Spectrum of Supply Chain Security
entry.content:
By Jay Thoden van Velzen, Technical Advisor, Office of the CSO

This blog discusses topics also covered in The Future of Supply Chain podcast, Episode 83: Supply Chain Security, Scope and Definitions. If you prefer podcasts over blogs, click the link!

 

Supply Chain Security From Different Viewpoints

There is increasing understanding that supply chain security is important to the resilience of business operations. However, since it covers many different aspects, we often misunderstand each other. The problem is that supply chain security means very different things depending on your functional area and from which viewpoint you look at it. Supply chain security covers a broad spectrum, and the term is used for widely different challenges. This leads to confusion, and people using metrics from one area to make an argument in another. It is not that simple.

To an operational business executive keeping a product line going, supply chain security refers to the physical movement of goods and components. To enterprise IT leaders, it is who and how contractors access company systems and facilities, and what technology providers they bring in. To software developers, supply chain security refers to their software dependency chain. When they discuss their supply chain challenges, they will have trouble understanding each other.

In all cases, we’re dealing with suppliers. But the reduction of risk in each area requires a very different approach. SAP, as a provider of business cloud solutions, touches on the entire spectrum of supply chain security. Hopefully, our broad experience can help get our three leaders on the same page.

 

Surveying the Spectrum

In broad outlines, we can distinguish between three main categories:

  • Physical Supply Chain Security
  • Digital Supply Chain Security
  • Software Supply Chain Security

The cases mentioned above fit rather neatly into those categories. However, there is also significant overlap between these categories, and it is therefore better to refer to supply chain security as a continuing spectrum. Just how in a color spectrum we can still distinguish between red, yellow or blue even though the scale is continuous, so are these three markers still useful to help us navigate the field.

Physical Supply Chain Security

At the outer edge of the spectrum, we see the risks to the supply chain with direct physical, material impact. Resilience must be built into the supply chain to withstand the impact of conflict, natural disasters, or sanctions. In recent years, we have seen pandemic restrictions on different regions at different times have significant cascading impact on supply chains. Conflicts have made major transport routes less safe or cut them off. Sanctions have forced re-alignment of energy supply lines and geopolitical tensions have forced relocation of semiconductor manufacturing and restricted technology transfer- and export regimes.

Another example in the physical realm is product tampering. How are products – whether supplies or finished products –protected from being tampered with and protected from theft in storage depots and during transit? Even if you have taken appropriate measures in your own facilities, inevitably there are spots in your supply chain you don’t have direct control over.

Aside from tampering, a similar problem comes up with component integrity. Not all suppliers operate with the same integrity, and may offer common electronic components, for instance, that are not up to standard or code. Using such components in consumer electronics can have health and safety consequences through malfunction.

Digital Supply Chain Security

Component integrity is also a digital problem. Such components are listed on a digital marketplace, with seemingly correct standard dimensions and specification, offered by a company that changes its name every 6 months. Verifying that the marketplace performs due diligence on the listed suppliers and their product offerings falls under digital supply chain security.

A key digital supply chain security challenge is the provisioning and monitoring of access to your organization’s systems, networks and facilities by third party contractors and service providers. These can be employees of a system integrator that help manage IT systems but also includes third party facility maintenance or cleaning staff. Such contractors are often issued access badges and credentials to internal systems to conduct their work. Others are treated as visitors and may need to be always accompanied by an employee host.

For managed service providers (MSP) and Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) scenarios, the dynamic reverses. Instead of provisioning access to third-party staff to systems you control, now your organization uses systems operated by a third-party supplier. This could be a transition from self-hosted e-mail servers operated by contractors to a cloud-based e-mail service, or from on-premise SAP ERP systems to SAP cloud ERP services.

Software Supply Chain Security

The final category covers the software that your organization chooses to use or include into your products and services. This runs from SaaS through Platform- and Infrastructure-as-a-Service (PaaS, IaaS), to commercial software solutions deployed in your IT landscape. This requires verification that such suppliers meet your security and compliance requirements, have effective security programs in place, and fix vulnerabilities and misconfigurations within an acceptable time frame.

It also includes any open source software (OSS) solutions that you use in your organization, or libraries you include in your own development projects. OSS components often help reduce cost and accelerate development, but they come with their own risks. Only the largest OSS projects can match the level of support commercial providers offer for their solutions. Strictly speaking, there is no supplier in OSS, unless you select an enterprise license or service contract. Many OSS components themselves have dependencies on other OSS projects. Managing OSS dependency chains and keeping components up to date yourself require significant effort that is often underestimated.

Software supply chain concerns have raised to the level of governmental policy and regulation.

 

Mitigating Supply Chain Security Risks

Leaving aside any physical security measures, we can reduce supply chain security risks with these mitigation approaches:

  • Business Planning and Supply Chain Resilience
  • Third Party Risk Management (TPRM) and Compliance
  • IAM and Zero Trust
  • Vulnerability and Patch Management

As shown in the diagram below, they apply differently across the risk spectrum.

Business Planning and Supply Chain Resilience

Business Planning and Supply Chain Resilience – and planning for supply chain resilience – is critical to manage an ever-shifting portfolio of suppliers of goods and services across different jurisdictions, customs controls, and tax regimes. Security risks to the supply chain must be included alongside other business risks in business planning, while resilience must be baked into the supply chain to have the agility to adjust when circumstances beyond your control require you to.

SAP has solutions in its portfolio, such as SAP Integrated Business Planning for Supply Chain and other SAP Supply Chain Management Software, SAP Business Network, or SAP International Trade Management, that can help with this.

The bar for this in the diagram extends to everywhere in the supply chain where we have suppliers, as well as the impact of geopolitics.

Third Party Risk Management and Compliance

TPRM and compliance verification are necessary to ensure that your goods and service providers conduct business in a way that meets your security and compliance requirements, as well as risk appetite. This includes the verification of audit reports from suppliers, but also the maturity of their security programs. It covers the integrity and reliability of the supplier and its leaders, adherence to ethical business practices, and the supplier’s likelihood to deliver on its commitments. Such assessments extend to the supplier’s suppliers, as well. Ultimately, we must decide whether any supplier is one you’re willing to do business with.

The bar for this in the diagram extends to everywhere in the supply chain where we have suppliers.

IAM and Zero Trust

IAM and Zero Trust governs identity and access to internal systems as well as managed services and cloud applications, both for employees and contractors. Zero Trust involves features such as Multi Factor Authentication (MFA), device identity, and network-, attribute- and policy-based access controls. The topic goes far beyond the scope of this blog, but the NIST Special Publication 800-207 on Zero Trust Architecture is a readable introduction and guide.

The bar in the diagram for this extends further to the right than in the previous two, despite the lack of suppliers in open source. This is to cover OSS applications or solutions you have built yourself from OSS but still need included in your authentication and authorization scope. It starts where you provide access to third parties to your systems and facilities.

Vulnerability and Patch Management

For whatever software you use, whether managed by a service provider or operated yourself, you have a responsibility for vulnerability and patch management – be it with increasing responsibilities the further right in the spectrum we go. For MSPs and SaaS solutions, you have an obligation to verify that your suppliers are meeting their commitments. For commercial and open source software you operate yourself, you are fully responsible for keeping the software up to date when patches become available, and that the software is configured securely.

Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) is often associated with software supply chain management, but I think it is better associated with Third Party Risk Management and Compliance. After all, it is intended to give customers a means to conduct their risk assessment in using the software or service of that supplier.  

For the provider of the software or service, the key strategy is Vulnerability and Patch Management. If those processes work well, the SBOM – including its software dependencies – will be of better quality and therefore make a stronger impression on your customers in their TPRM evaluation.

 

Cross-Functional Effort

From these approaches, it is clear that these will be covered by different functional areas in the organization. Business planning falls under business operations. TPRM and compliance under Governance, Risk and Compliance (GRC), while IAM and Zero Trust and vulnerability management cover IT, cybersecurity and development teams. Covering supply chain security risk is therefore a cross-functional effort.

None of these approaches are easy. But there are suppliers, including SAP, that can support you in that journey.

 

More Information

More information can be found through the following links:

  • The Future of Supply Chain podcast, Episode 83: Supply Chain Security, Scope and Definitions
  • Government’s Role in Increasing Software Supply Chain Security: A Toolbox for Policy Makers (interface, March 2023)
  • SAP Supply Chain Management Software
  • SAP Business Network
  • SAP International Trade Management
  • NIST Special Publication 800-207 on Zero Trust Architecture

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: Connect the dots at the 2025 SAP GRC conference in Budapest!
entry.content:
Once again we are proud to be working with TAC Insights to bring together our two popular GRC conferences on internal control, compliance and risk management and on cybersecurity and data protection into a one stop shop event for all Governance, Risk, and Compliance & Security professionals. SAP for Internal Controls, Compliance and Risk Management is the not-to-be-missed event for business and IT professionals in the SAP GRC ecosystem: customers, prospects, and partners.

 

What can you expect?

Let’s just start with a few facts:

  • 150+ delegates from across a wide range of industries, representing SAP customers, prospects, partners, and solution experts
  • Highly interactive format with opportunities for 1:1 meetings, round tables, solution demos and more
  • Exclusive, business focussed insights, actionable solutions and lessons learned directly from established SAP customers

 

Insightful keynotes

In the conference opening keynote Connect the dots, Axel Vetter – Head of Quote-to-Cash & Governance, Risk Management, and Compliance Product Marketing at SAP, will share his perspective on what the new risk landscape looks like, and what SAP is doing to help companies bring out their best. This of course includes sharing the highlights of SAP’s roadmap for GRC & Security solutions.

We will then have a keynote by the event’s platinum sponsor delivered by Richard Hunt – Founder and CEO Turnkey Consulting, and Chris Johnston – Head of Office of the CFO Advisory for EMEA SAP, on the topic of People, protection, performance: elevating security as an enabler of business growth. Richard and Chris will challenge common misconceptions about the business value of security and showcase real world examples of what’s possible when people, protection, and performance come together.

 

Real life case studies from users

Some examples of real-life use cases from companies across industries and geographies that will be presented during the course of these 2 days include:

 

Shell

An exploration of the company’s ongoing journey from manual controls to digitally enabled efficient controls through SAP Process Control.

 

Focusing on how Siemens Healthineers protects its critical SAP assets proactively in a global environment on its transformation to SAP RISE with the help of Onapsis.

Siemens Healthineers

 

Postnord

An overview of Postnord’s technology landscape, its SAP implementation process of SAP Identity Access Governance, the resources used, and a detailed insight into “how they did it”.

 

How, by leveraging SAP Audit Management and SAP Process Control, internal audit departments can enhance the efficiency of audit processes, improve the risk-based audits, and simplify the follow-up on actions.

OYAK Mining Metallurgy

 

CSM Ingredients

Getting behind the scenes of CSM Ingredients’ move from SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA with RISE, this session will provide highlights on how CSM Ingredients saved on license costs, reduced risks, and improved compliance by focusing on SAP security.

 

As a follow-up from last year’s session, the presenter will explain how L’Oréal aligned its business processes with group directives through a business authorization model implemented in its Accounting and Finance shared service centers, and during the deployment of the group’s IGA tool.

L’Oréal

 

BP

BP is a regular presenter at our conference and always shares highly appreciated insights on the various aspects of their processes and tools. This year, BP will cover its journey of meeting its key Internal Control over Finance Reporting (ICFR) obligations and automating key control requirements using SAP GRC and how the team has transformed the control monitoring in real time across all business processes, running close to hundreds of business rules for as many control activities across the group.

 

This session will address Perfetti Van Melle’s journey from a reactive and manual GRC Maturity Model to an integrated, proactive and automated approach supported by SAP Access Control, SAP Process Control and SAP Audit Management solutions

Perfetti Van Melle

 

Wipro Enterprises

Explaining how strategic focus on role design and access governance paved the way for improved risk management and sustained compliance, this session will provide insights into the steps, approaches, and lessons that contributed to building a resilient governance structure aligned with modern business needs.

 

SAP and partner led solution roadmaps, strategic updates and deep dives

 

Riscomp

Maxim Chupronov – Principal consultant and CEO of Riscomp and Vincent Doux – GRC & Security Customer Solution Advisor for EMEA at SAP, will provide an overview of SAP cybersecurity risk management and compliance, with a focus on key concepts like NIS2.

 

A regular feature of the conference, the “What’s New” sessions for SAP solutions for Three Lines and for Identity Access Management and Cybersecurity & Data Protection are back. With enhancements delivered regularly, it's easy to miss useful updates. SAP experts will provide the audience with a rapid overview of new capabilities recently delivered, as well as explanations and illustrations of select new features and functionality.

SAP

 

Where and when?

If you feel this agenda would be relevant to you, then join us on site!

  • Dates: March 11-12, 2025
  • Location: Budapest, Hungary
  • Venue: Marriott Budapest

 

How?

Well, that’s the easy part! Just head to the event website and registration page:

  • Event home page
  • Interactive agenda
  • Get your tickets!

 

Still not sure?

If you are still not sure if this is the right event, then allow me to share some of the great feedback that we have heard from last year:

I hope this gave you a taste for what is to come in Budapest, and I look forward to meeting you there. In the meantime, feel free to share your thoughts and comments either on this blog or on Twitter @TFrenehard

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

entry.title: Enhancement - Risk analysis to consider or dismiss empty(Dummy) values in authorizations
entry.content:
The Purpose of this Blog is to explain the enhancement for considering or dismissing(exclude) Empty (Dummy) authorization values in GRC Risk Analysis.

We observe that the risk analysis engine does not consider the empty (dummy) authorization values maintained in the authorization objects (like object S_USER_GRP field CLASS with value ' ') starting from GRC AC 12 SP16. The most probable symptom is the disappearance of some permission level violations.

Before AC 12.0 SP16, the risk analysis considered the empty authorization values (dummy values), but with the introduction of SP16, they automatically became excluded. SAP has come up with the enhancement after we reporting this issue. With this enhancement you can decide how the risk analysis should handle the empty(Dummy) authorization values. You can either restore the pre-SP16 behavior or go with the current one by excluding Empty authorizations.

Solution is : Please implement the attached Correction Instruction in the note 3482508 or upgrade to the equivalent Support Package. Please be informed that you also need to perform the Manual Activities (if the objects are not created by Snote) after the Note implementation.

A new SPRO parameter has been introduced: 1056 Consideration type for empty authorization values

Path: SPRO--> Governance, Risk and Compliance --> Access Control-->Maintain Configuration Settings

This parameter decides whether the risk analysis considers or skips the empty values in the authorizations.

Possible values for the SPRO 1056 parameter:


If set to '1' -> empty auth values will NOT be considered (risk analysis behaves as of SP16)
If set to '2' -> empty auth values will be considered (risk analysis behaves as worked before SP16)

entry.title: Defending Against AI Security Risks by Turning LLMs on LLMs
entry.content:
By Slim Trabelsi, Head of AI, Product Security, SAP Global Security and Cloud Compliance Product Security and Jay Thoden van Velzen, Technical Advisor, Office of the CSO

As a provider of both Business AI solutions and the AI Core platform that provides access to third parties and open source Large Language Models (LLMs), SAP is committed to Responsible AI. Part of that commitment is to continuously look for ways to protect against AI security risks.

SAP’s approach to only train with anonymized training data and use RAG and Business Connectors to systems with access control prevents most data leakage and privacy risks. However, there are other security risks that are not covered by that. We still must test the model behavior against the most common AI vulnerabilities:

  • Prompt injection, or coercing LLMs into unexpected behavior
  • Jail breaking, that is, bypassing safety measures built into the model
  • System prompt leaks where a model reveals its internal instructions

All LLMs are vulnerable to such attacks as they are to some degree. Guardrails and system prompts can be applied to mitigate against attacks, by validating input and output, as well as system prompts that instruct the LLM to restrict itself to approved responses. But to know what to defend against, you first need to know which attacks an LLM is vulnerable to. The models are updated constantly, and new attacks are identified continuously as well.

 

STARS

In 2023, a team in SAP’s Product Security organization started a project to see if testing for such AI vulnerabilities could be automated. SAP provides a wide variety of models through SAP AI Core, and the speed of development and proliferation of new models dictated that we had to scale up testing quickly. Specifically, the team wanted to see if LLMs could be leveraged to test LLMs for these vulnerabilities.

The initial proof of concept was promising, and the project developed into the Smart Threat AI Reporting Scanner, or STARS. This scanner tests all models that are provided on AI Core, as well as other models SAP evaluates internally, and is integrated in SAP’s secure development practices. It uses a GenAI agent to inject a variety of attacks into the LLM being tested to try to coerce it into bad behavior. To do that it uses two types of tool libraries to provide it with different attack types:

  • Static libraries with 1-step prompt injections
  • Dynamic, multi-step libraries

In the static attacks, the scanner runs through a series of common prompt injection attacks. A good example is the “ignore all previous instructions” attack, followed by an otherwise banned request, in the hope that the LLM would indeed respond to that and bypass instructions built into the model. The scanner plays each attack and records whether it succeeded or not.

The dynamic attacks take longer, and use LLMs in multi-step attacks, maintaining an ongoing conversation with the model under attack. The attack adapts to the response of the LLM and continues to coerce the model into bad behavior. It hooks into anywhere why the model gives a response that looks promising, and iterates to the point where the LLM misbehaves, similar to what happens in fuzzing. These tests, understandably, take for more time to run.

New attack libraries can be added as they become available, to add them to the body of test cases STARS runs through.

A Smart Judge

The results of the tests are passed on to a Judge to evaluate whether the attack was successful or not. The tool libraries reported some attacks as successful when actually they were not. The response might have been unexpected but was not considered harmful. To reduce false positives and confirm that an attack really succeeded in triggering a malicious response, the team developed their own Judge on top of an LLM. This Judge model is independent of the model that is being assessed, as well as the injection method (either static or dynamic).

Each LLM being tested is run through 10 tests in a row to evaluate its behavior. The response is not always the same, so the repeated tests gives a sense how often the model might misbehave under a particular attack. This ultimately results in a risk score for the LLM being tested that can be compared to others or to different versions of the same model. The results are displayed in a heat map showing which level and type of attack a model is vulnerable to. For instance, if the model is vulnerable to 1-step static attacks, it is likely to receive a high risk score.

The relative risks scores of LLMs are shared with developer teams to consider. They evaluate the security risk score along with ethics and safety tests, and suitability of the model for the developer team’s business use case. Considered together they can make an informed decision which LLM to use in their product. For a variety of reasons, SAP can’t disclose these security risk scores. However, STARS is now released as open source, so organizations can run their own model evaluations, whether against models available on SAP AI Core or otherwise.

Diagram showing STARS and its LLM model targets, as well as providing training data for LLM Defense

LLM Defense

It is good to get a sense of the relative security risks of using an LLM. It is even better to mitigate against them. A separate project aims to do that with LLM Defense, which is an inline solution that leverages GenAI to perform input and output sanitation on prompts and responses. Successful attacks in SMART are used to train LLM Defense to detect such attacks and defend against them. This takes three forms:

  1. Static patterns to detect attacks
  2. Pre-prompt reminder to the LLM to behave according to its system prompt
  3. Dynamic detection to identify tentative input and suspect output that diverges from the expected context

Static patterns protect primarily against 1-step static attack patterns. The pre-prompt reminder re-enforces the system prompt and instructs the model again “stick to that”. LLMs can sometimes apply only parts of their system prompt – especially when it is quite long – and such a pre-prompt reminder helps the LLM not to miss an important control. The dynamic detection looks for multi-step attacks that succeeded during SMART testing and blocks prompts or responses that look like that.

LLM Defense is deployed on BTP as an SAP internal-only self-service API.

 

Defenders’ Advantage

As others do, SAP believes that, while adversaries will use LLMs to their advantage, LLMs provides defenders on balance an advantage. Tools such as STARS and LLM Defense harnessing LLMs for defense help to stay ahead of adversaries trying to attack AI solutions. SAP is now automating STARS to immediately scan any new model that is added to SAP AI Core, and train LLM Defense before adversaries may be able to interact with the AI Business Solution.

We encourage you to try out STARS for yourself, and welcome contributions to the project.

 

More Information

STARS is released as open source. There are currently no plans to do the same for LLM Defense.

  • STARS Github repository
  • Secure, Empower, Advance – How AI Can Reverse the Defender’s Dilemma, Google, Feb 2024

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: What's New in SAP Risk and Assurance Management, Q4 2024 to Q1 2025
entry.content:
By moving to continuous delivery for our SaaS solutions – including of course SAP Risk and Assurance Management, SAP Product Management and Engineering teams are aiming at addressing 3 main benefits:

1. Deliveries without disruption: updates will not interfere with the daily work of end users and no regression tests are necessary

2. Flexible adoption of innovations: continuous deliveries consist of pre-delivered new functionalities usually delivered behind toggles to give customers the flexibility to actively decide whether to put a functionality into operation straight away or to wait until the releases following the update as it then becomes available to all customers by default

3. Faster action on feedback or improvement requests: this approach allows SAP to innovate faster and respond more quickly to improvement requests.

Thanks to this delivery strategy, there have been quite a few recent enhancements for SAP Risk and Assurance Management, and I have selected just a few below to highlight. This is by no means the entire list!

 

New risk analysis methods

 

Quantitative risk assessment was already available in SAP Risk and Assurance Management since the delivery of the Risk Service module in Q2 2024, but new risk analysis methods are now also available: Qualitative Analysis and Mixed Analysis.

In Qualitative Analysis, users can estimate an amount of financial loss incurred and the likelihood of occurrence for the risk. The total loss is derived from the sum of the quantitative impacts and the amount of loss is automatically translated into an impact level and impact score against the impact scale defined by the organization in the configuration settings of the solution.

Mixed Analysis is used when there is a combination of both qualitative and quantitative assessments. In this approach, the Total Loss is not used, but the Total Impact Level and Total Impact Score are. Here, the sum of the quantitative impacts is translated into an impact level based on the impact scale, and then compared with the qualitative impacts. The highest impact level is taken as the total impact level.

Summary of what is available when:

 

Integration with SAP Signavio for Controls and Risks version 2.0

 

Last year, SAP Product Management and Engineering had delivered the integration between SAP Signavio and SAP Risk and Assurance Management with an import of all processes from the first one to the second one. Processes where then synchronised between the 2 solutions and there was also another integration that related to controls. Controls could be created in SAP Risk and Assurance Management and exported to SAP Signavio where they could be assigned to process tasks.

This was integration 1.0.

We now have version 2.0 of this integration with much broader capability since the risk is also included in the scope. Risks can therefore be pushed from SAP Risk and Assurance Management to SAP Signavio.

But there’s more: legacy controls and risks from SAP Signavio to SAP Risk and Assurance Management are also now supported. So, if an organization has been using SAP Signavio for some time and has already populated controls and risks there and they now start using SAP Risk and Assurance Management, they can push their controls and risks from SAP Signavio to SAP Risk and Assurance Management.

Version 2.0 of this integration therefore enables customers to use SAP Risk and Assurance Management as a single central repository for creating and managing risks and controls, and to export them to SAP Signavio on a regular basis.

 

Automatic Issue Owner Determination, Notification, and Assignment

 

With this new capability, when a new issue is created, the system can automatically identify the person who will be in charge of its resolution. This is the issue owner.

With automatic dispatching of new issues to designated users and email notifications to make nominees aware of it, issue owners are notified in a timely manner so they can’t really miss it.

This enhancement further helps issues find their best processor under consideration of absences, etc. and avoid orphaned issues by automatically assigning an issue owner after a grace period.

To do so, there is an app Manage Teams and Responsibilities where you can create the teams based on determination rules and define who gets the action items.

The process to determine the right stakeholder before was manual so every new issue had to be manually dispatched. Now, the solution takes care of it automatically and makes it more convenient... and scalable!

 

GRC Data Consolidation Service

 

Dashboard and reports are key components in any efficient Compliance program. Not only to be able to communicate results to Senior Management, but also to be able to monitor control results and issue status.

In this area, the new GRC Data Consolidation service provides an API to replicate consolidated information for controls and issues enabling organizations to visualize this data in charts and graphics in any analytical tool that can use OData. Including SAP Analytics Cloud of course.

Below are just some examples of use cases of data from SAP Risk and Assurance Management that can be visualized:

What’s more, semantic fields can also be leveraged:

 

Looking for additional enhancements and more details?

 

As mentioned in the introduction, this is only a sample of the recent enhancements. But you will be able to find the complete list as well as further documentation directly in the dedicated section of the SAP Help Portal: What's New in SAP Risk and Assurance Management

I look forward to reading your thoughts and comments either on this blog or on Twitter @TFrenehard

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

entry.title: Chart of Accounts Optimization: The First Step in Your Cloud-Ready Finance Transformation
entry.content:
Chart of Accounts Optimization: The First Step in Your Cloud-Ready Finance Transformation

Introduction

As businesses embark on their digital transformation journeys, finance leaders often find themselves at a crossroads—how to ensure their financial structures are ready for the cloud. The answer lies in optimizing the Chart of Accounts (CoA) as the first and most crucial step. An inefficient CoA can slow down reporting, complicate compliance, and hinder real-time decision-making.

On the other hand, a well-structured CoA serves as the backbone of financial transparency, automation, and scalability—making it a key enabler of cloud transformation. With SAP S/4HANA Cloud becoming the standard for enterprise finance, businesses must rethink their legacy CoA structures to ensure they are lean, scalable, and intelligent.

In this blog, we’ll explore the need for CoA optimization, introduce the SMART framework, and outline a three-step advisory roadmap to get started.

Why CoA Optimization is the First Step in Moving to the Cloud : Many organizations overlook the importance of CoA in cloud transformation. However, migrating to a cloud-based ERP with an outdated or bloated CoA can lead to inefficiencies rather than improvements.

An unstructured CoA can lead to:

❌Redundant GL accounts, making reporting complex

❌Manual reconciliations, slowing financial closing

❌Non-compliant structures, increasing audit risks

❌Inconsistent CoA across business units, causing mismatches

CoA is the Fundamental for Finance

Here’s why optimizing CoA is a non-negotiable first step:

1. Standardization for Global Operations :

A streamlined CoA eliminates redundant accounts and ensures uniform financial reporting across business units and geographies. In cloud environments, a harmonized CoA reduces complexity in multi-entity reporting.

Before Optimization: • Multiple GL accounts for similar transactions across business units (e.g., 5000 - Sales USA, 6000 - Sales Europe). • Inconsistencies in reporting structures lead to complex financial consolidation.

After Optimization: • Harmonized CoA across all entities (e.g., 5000 - Sales Revenue, with dimensions for country-wise reporting).

• Consolidated reporting becomes automated, reducing reconciliation time by 40%.

Sample CoA Structure

Result: A leaner, standardized CoA allows for real-time reporting, compliance automation, and AI-powered financial insights.

2. Real-Time Financial Insights

A well-structured CoA enables SAP S/4HANA’s Universal Journal, allowing finance teams to get real-time insights instead of batch-processed reports. This is critical for businesses leveraging predictive analytics and AI-driven decision-making.

Before Optimization: • Sales and cost data scattered across multiple accounts, requiring manual adjustments. • No direct visibility into profitability at product or region level.

After Optimization: • Optimized CoA structured for real-time tracking via SAP Universal Journal. • AI-driven insights allow predictive sales analytics, improving demand forecasting.

3. Regulatory Compliance & Audit Readiness: 

Cloud-based finance systems need to comply with multiple regulations (IFRS, GAAP, tax laws). An optimized CoA ensures compliance with built-in financial controls, reducing audit complexities.

Before Optimization: • GL accounts not aligned with IFRS/GAAP, requiring manual intervention during audits. • High risk of non-compliance due to region-specific accounting structures.

After Optimization: • CoA aligned with global and local accounting standards. • AI-based anomaly detection flags potential compliance risks in real-time.

4. Seamless Integration Across Finance & Operations  Finance is no longer an isolated function—it interacts with logistics, sales, HR, and procurement. A clean CoA structure ensures smooth data flow across all modules in SAP, enabling automation and intelligent processing.

Before Optimization: • Finance, logistics, and sales maintained separate CoA structures, causing data mismatches. • Month-end closing delayed due to manual reconciliation efforts.

After Optimization: • Unified CoA across finance and operations, allowing real-time integration with procurement and sales. • AI-powered auto-reconciliation reduces closing time by 50%.

5. AI & Automation Readiness :

 AI-powered financial automation (auto-reconciliation, predictive analytics) depends on clean, structured data. A well-optimized CoA allows machine learning models to operate efficiently, reducing manual effort in financial operations.

Before Optimization: • Legacy CoA with excessive manual processes for financial analysis. • Error-prone data hinders AI adoption for automation.

After Optimization: • AI-optimized CoA structure allows for automated journal entries and predictive financial forecasting. • AI-driven fraud detection reduces financial risks by 20%.

With these factors in mind, organizations must approach CoA optimization with a structured framework.

This is where SMART  comes into play.

Introducing the SMART  Framework for CoA Optimization The SMART framework provides a structured methodology for designing a lean, scalable, and AI-ready CoA for the future.

SMART – Core Optimization for CoA

🔹S – Standardization & Simplification

✅Reduce redundant GL accounts and align CoA structures across entities.

✅Use AI-driven tools to analyze transaction patterns and eliminate unused accounts.

✅Before Optimization: Multiple redundant GL accounts (e.g., 10001 - Advertising USA, 10002 - Advertising UK).

✅After Optimization: One standard GL (1000 - Advertising Expense) with dimensions for country-wise tracking. 📌 Use Case: A global FMCG firm reduced its GL accounts by 40% using AI-based CoA mapping, accelerating financial consolidation.

Journal Entry Example:

🔻Before Optimization

(Legacy CoA with Redundant Accounts):

Dr. 601100 - Print Marketing Expense $10,000

Cr. 200100 - Accounts Payable $10,000

🔹After Optimization

(Standardized CoA with Dimensions):

Dr. 601000 - Marketing Expense (Print) $10,000

Cr. 200100 - Accounts Payable $10,000

🔹M – Modernization for Digital Finance

✅Leverage S/4HANA’s Universal Journal for real-time reporting and insights.

✅Use AI for automated anomaly detection in financial reporting.

✅Before Optimization: Delayed insights due to batch processing of financial data.

✅After Optimization: AI-driven financial reporting with real-time cost tracking.

🔻Before Optimization (Manual Revenue Recognition):

Dr. 120000 - Accounts Receivable $50,000

Cr. 400001 - Sales Revenue USA $50,000

🔹After Optimization (AI-Powered Real-Time CoA with Dimensions):

Dr. 120000 - Accounts Receivable $50,000

Cr. 400000 - Sales Revenue (Region: USA, Product: Electronics) $50,000

📌Use Case: A manufacturing company used AI to flag unusual cost variances, reducing financial errors by 25%.

🔹A – Alignment with Compliance & Regulatory Needs

✅Map CoA to IFRS, GAAP, and tax regulations, ensuring global compliance.

✅Automate regulatory reporting using AI-based compliance monitoring.

✅Before Optimization: Different CoA structures across regions, leading to compliance challenges.

✅After Optimization: AI-powered real-time audit trails and automated regulatory reporting.

Journal Entry Example:

🔻Before Optimization (Manually Adjusted Depreciation Accounting):

Dr. 650010 - Depreciation Expense (Old CoA) $5,000

Cr. 170000 - Accumulated Depreciation $5,000

🔹After Optimization (IFRS-Compliant AI-Driven CoA):

Dr. 650000 - Depreciation Expense (IFRS) $5,000

Cr. 170000 - Accumulated Depreciation $5,000

📌Use Case: A multinational bank reduced compliance efforts by 50% through AI-driven reporting automation.

🔹R – Real-time Insights & Reporting

✅Structure CoA to enable predictive analytics and AI-powered financial forecasting.

✅Use embedded SAP analytics for real-time tracking of business performance.

✅Before Optimization: Fragmented revenue recognition across different CoA structures.

✅After Optimization: AI-powered CoA with real-time revenue forecasting.

Journal Entry Example:

🔻Before Optimization (Manually Categorized Cost of Goods Sold - COGS):

Dr. 500001 - Raw Material Cost $30,000

Dr. 500002 - Labor Cost $20,000

Cr. 210000 - Accounts Payable $50,000

🔹After Optimization (AI-Enhanced COGS Categorization in Universal Journal):

Dr. 500000 - COGS (Material: Steel, Region: APAC) $50,000

Cr. 210000 - Accounts Payable $50,000

📌Use Case: A retail firm leveraged SKU-level revenue tracking to optimize pricing and inventory planning.

🔹T – Tailored for Future Scalability

✅Ensure flexibility for M&A, new business models, and rapid expansions.

✅Use AI-based simulations to test post-merger financial structures.

✅Before Optimization: Rigid CoA structure unable to accommodate new business models.

✅After Optimization: AI-assisted CoA simulations for mergers, acquisitions, and expansions.

Journal Entry Example:

🔻Before Optimization (Different CoA Structures Across Merged Companies):

Dr. 702010 - M&A Costs - Company A $100,000

Dr. 800010 - Acquisition Costs - Company B $100,000
